critic_raw: The RNN with attention mechanisms for patient readmission prediction has three key methodological concerns: temporal leakage from random split on clinical time-series, recall as a gameable standalone metric, and conceptual error in using hospital/demographic identifiers as features to 'control confounds.'
all_issues_raised: Stratified random split on 3-year clinical admission data assumes weak temporal autocorrelation without validation
Recall as standalone primary metric is gameable—any positive-predicting model achieves 100% recall
Including demographic and hospital identifiers as features encodes protected attributes and does not control confounds

round_transcripts.critic: The RNN with attention mechanisms for patient readmission prediction has three key methodological concerns: temporal leakage from random split on clinical time-series, recall as a gameable standalone metric, and conceptual error in using hospital/demographic identifiers as features to 'control confounds.'
round_transcripts.defender: On recall as primary metric: the design states 'a focus on recall, selected as the primary metric, aligns with stakeholders' emphasis on identifying as many true readmissions as possible.' While recall alone is gameable, the design also includes 'precision, F1-score, and AUROC' as supportive metrics, and model selection would practically be informed by the combination. The design's intent is clear: maximize identification of true readmissions while using secondary metrics to prevent degenerate solutions. The protocol is imperfect but operationally motivated. On identifiers as features: the design says 'including demographic and hospital identifiers within the feature set' to account for 'variability across patient populations and procedural differences.' If hospital identifiers are included as features rather than stratification variables, the model may learn hospital-specific patterns—the question is whether this is desirable (capturing real hospital-level predictive information) or problematic (confounding by coding practice). This is a genuine design tension. On temporal split: the assumption of 'sufficiently weak' temporal autocorrelation over three years of clinical data is empirically questionable.
round_transcripts.defender_continued: The temporal split concern is substantive and unresolved.

round_transcripts.critic: All three issues remain. On recall: 'supportive metrics' do not govern model selection—the design explicitly says recall is the primary metric. A model with 99% recall but 1% precision would be selected over a model with 85% recall and 70% precision under this protocol. On identifiers as features: the defense acknowledges the tension but frames it as a design choice. However, using race/demographic as a feature in a clinical prediction model is a fairness concern that goes beyond model performance—it embeds protected attributes into predictions and can perpetuate or amplify existing health disparities. This should not be presented as a neutral 'design tension.' On temporal split: no substantive defense was offered.
round_transcripts.defender: The defender concedes all three concerns. Recall as standalone primary metric requires a precision floor constraint. Hospital identifiers and demographic features in the feature set require careful fairness analysis and should not be included without explicit fairness evaluation. Temporal split should be time-based for clinical cohort data.

all_issues_adjudicated: Stratified random split on 3-year clinical admission data assumes weak temporal autocorrelation without validation
Recall as standalone primary metric is gameable—any positive-predicting model achieves 100% recall
Including demographic and hospital identifiers as features encodes protected attributes and does not control confounds
